_meta.authoringNotes: Classification per Spitzer Quebec Task Force 1995 (J Manipulative Physiol Ther 1995). Treatment per Bone and Joint Decade Neck Pain Task Force 2008 (PMID 18204390), NICE NG56 (2023 update). Epidemiology per Carroll et al. Spine 2008 (PMID 18204389) and OECD road injury statistics. Exercise evidence per Sterling 2014 (PMID 24388208). Canadian C-spine Rule per Stiell 2003 (PMID 14534336).

h1Title: Whiplash Injury: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Whiplash injury is a soft-tissue neck injury caused by rapid forced hyperextension followed by flexion of the cervical spine, most often during a rear-end motor vehicle collision. Incidence in motorised countries is roughly 300-600 per 100,000 person-years and the condition accounts for the largest share of soft-tissue injury claims after road traffic collisions. The classic syndrome is neck pain and stiffness developing within 24-48 hours of impact, often radiating to the upper back, shoulders, or head, with reduced cervical range of motion and tender paraspinal muscles. About 50% of patients recover fully within 3-6 months, but 25-30% develop persistent symptoms beyond a year (chronic whiplash-associated disorder), a major driver of healthcare cost and disability. Early reassurance, advice to stay active, simple analgesics, and structured exercise outperform collars and prolonged rest in modern guidelines.

keyStats.prevalence: 300-600 per 100,000 person-years in high-income motorised countries; up to 1,000 per 100,000 where rear-end collision rates are higher (Carroll et al. Spine 2008)
keyStats.demographics: Female:male ratio approximately 1.5-2:1; peak ages 20-49 years
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at first whiplash injury 30-40 years
keyStats.globalCases: Conservative estimate of 2-3 million new cases per year worldwide; the leading cause of soft-tissue claims following road traffic collisions

definition: Whiplash injury (ICD-10: S13.4, S13.9), formally Whiplash-Associated Disorder (WAD), describes a constellation of neck symptoms produced by a sudden acceleration-deceleration mechanism transferring energy to the cervical spine, most often during a rear-end or side-impact motor vehicle collision but also during sports, falls, and amusement-park rides. The forces stretch and partially tear capsular ligaments of the facet joints (C5-C6 and C6-C7 are most often affected), strain paraspinal muscles, and may injure the cervical intervertebral discs. The Quebec Task Force on Whiplash-Associated Disorders (Spitzer 1995) graded severity from 0 (no symptoms or signs) through I (pain, stiffness, no clinical signs), II (musculoskeletal signs), III (neurological signs), to IV (fracture or dislocation). Grades I-II represent the soft-tissue whiplash typically managed in primary care and rehabilitation, while grades III-IV require orthopaedic or neurosurgical assessment. Persistent symptoms beyond 3 months define chronic WAD. The disease is managed by general practice, emergency medicine, physical medicine and rehabilitation, physiotherapy, sports medicine, and occasionally orthopaedics or neurosurgery.

typesClassification.description: No neck complaint and no physical signs. Often documented for medico-legal purposes after a low-speed collision without symptoms.
Neck pain, stiffness, or tenderness without musculoskeletal or neurological signs. Most common grade; the majority recover within 4-6 weeks with active care.
Neck pain plus musculoskeletal signs — restricted range of motion and point tenderness on examination. Higher rates of persistent symptoms than grade I.
Neck pain plus neurological signs — diminished reflexes, weakness, or sensory deficits suggesting cervical nerve-root involvement. Requires imaging and specialist review.
Neck pain plus radiologically confirmed fracture or dislocation. Requires emergency immobilisation, advanced imaging, and orthopaedic or neurosurgical management.

primarySymptoms: Neck pain and stiffness developing 6-48 hours after a rear-end or side-impact collision, often described as worse on waking the day after.
Reduced cervical range of motion, especially with rotation and extension, with paraspinal muscle spasm tender to palpation.
Occipital and upper-thoracic radiating pain reaching the shoulders, scapulae, and base of the skull (cervicogenic headache).
Headache, most commonly tension-type or cervicogenic, present in 50-70% of patients in the first month.
Jaw pain, clicking, or temporomandibular dysfunction in up to 25% of patients, particularly after high-energy impacts.
Dizziness, vertigo, or unsteadiness without true vestibular signs, often reflecting cervicogenic dizziness or autonomic involvement.
Tinnitus, hyperacusis, or sensation of fullness in the ears reported in 10-15% of patients.
Paraesthesia or transient pins-and-needles in the arms and hands, more concerning when persistent or accompanied by weakness (suggests grade III WAD).
Concentration difficulties, irritability, low mood, and sleep disturbance, increasing risk of chronic WAD.
earlyWarningSigns: Severe occipital headache and neck stiffness developing within hours of a high-speed collision
Persistent paraesthesia or arm weakness more than 48 hours after impact
Catastrophising beliefs about the injury and high baseline pain intensity (>6/10) which predict chronic disability
Pre-existing anxiety, depression, chronic pain, or post-traumatic stress disorder before the collision
Marked dizziness, visual disturbance, or unsteadiness in the first week
emergencySigns: Sudden severe neck pain with bilateral arm weakness or sensory level — exclude cervical fracture or spinal cord injury with urgent imaging
New bladder or bowel dysfunction after a cervical injury — possible spinal cord injury
Severe occipital headache with neck stiffness, vomiting, and focal neurology — exclude vertebral artery dissection or subarachnoid haemorrhage
Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) after a neck injury — possible carotid or vertebral artery dissection
Difficulty swallowing, hoarseness, or expanding neck swelling — possible retropharyngeal haematoma

causes.description: Roughly 60-80% of whiplash injuries follow rear-end collisions. Impact pushes the seat forward, accelerating the trunk while inertia keeps the head behind, producing rapid hyperextension followed by rebound flexion. Velocity changes (delta-V) as low as 5-10 km/h can produce symptoms in susceptible occupants.

Lateral and frontal crashes apply oblique acceleration vectors to the cervical spine, producing additional lateral flexion and rotation strain. These collisions account for the next 20-30% of cases and often result in higher WAD grades.

Contact sports (rugby, American football, ice hockey), horse riding falls, mountain biking crashes, and amusement-park rides can replicate the hyperextension-flexion mechanism. Boxing and mixed martial arts can produce repeated low-grade whiplash injuries.

Falls onto the head or upper back and assaults (punches, shaken-head mechanisms) produce a similar inertial cervical injury. Elderly fallers may sustain whiplash-like injury with lower-energy mechanisms because of reduced muscle tone.

Heavy machinery operation, sudden lifting overhead, and certain amusement rides (especially roller coasters with rapid head acceleration) account for a smaller share of cases. Documenting the mechanism is important for occupational and insurance reasons.

riskFactors.description: Women are 1.5-2 times more likely to sustain whiplash and 2 times more likely to develop chronic symptoms, partly attributed to smaller cervical muscle mass and longer neck-to-thorax ratio.
Highest incidence in working-age adults reflecting driving exposure. Older adults with degenerative cervical disease may have more severe outcomes from lower-energy impacts.

riskFactors.factor: Previous neck pain or whiplash injury
riskFactors.description: Patients with prior neck pain have 2-3 times higher risk of chronic WAD and slower recovery, even when imaging is normal.
Rear-end mechanism doubles the risk of whiplash compared with frontal impact at equivalent delta-V. Awareness of the collision (preparedness) is protective.
Rotation increases asymmetric loading of facet joints and muscles, raising the risk of higher-grade WAD and persistent symptoms.
Pre-collision anxiety, depression, or post-traumatic stress doubles the risk of chronic WAD. Catastrophising beliefs about injury are the strongest psychological predictor of persistent disability.

riskFactors.factor: High baseline pain intensity and disability
riskFactors.description: Initial pain intensity above 6/10 and Neck Disability Index above 30/100 at presentation predict chronic symptoms in 40-60% of cases.
Ongoing personal-injury litigation is associated with slower recovery in some cohorts, though the effect size is debated. Early case resolution and clinician advice favour recovery.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-49 years sustain 65-75% of whiplash injuries (Carroll et al. 2008).
Women account for 55-65% of whiplash claims and have higher rates of chronic WAD.
Countries with high vehicle ownership (Australia, Canada, Netherlands, UK, USA, Scandinavia, Japan) report incidence of 300-600 per 100,000 person-years.
Compensable injuries: rates rise where claims systems reimburse short-term disability, fall under no-fault systems.
Children and adolescents: rare under age 10; recovery is generally faster than adults of working age.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical. The history pinpoints the mechanism (rear-end, side-impact, fall), head position at impact, presence of seat-belt and headrest, immediate versus delayed symptom onset, and the trajectory of pain over the first 24-72 hours. Examination assesses cervical range of motion (active and passive), tenderness over the facet joints and paraspinal muscles, neurological status (myotomes, dermatomes, reflexes, Hoffmann sign, gait), and the temporomandibular and shoulder girdles. The Canadian C-spine Rule is the most widely validated tool for deciding which alert, stable adults need radiography after blunt cervical trauma: any high-risk factor (age above 65, dangerous mechanism, paraesthesia) mandates imaging; low-risk factors (simple rear-end collision, sitting in emergency department, ambulatory, delayed onset of pain, no midline tenderness) allow safe range-of-motion testing. Plain radiographs in three views suffice for most low-risk patients; CT cervical spine is preferred in older adults, severe trauma, and high-risk mechanism; MRI is reserved for neurological signs, persistent radicular symptoms, or planned intervention. Routine MRI in uncomplicated grade I-II WAD is not recommended. The Neck Disability Index (NDI) and a 0-10 numeric pain rating scale at baseline guide prognosis and treatment intensity. Psychological screening for catastrophising, hyperarousal, and post-traumatic stress is increasingly recommended where access permits.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with the Canadian C-spine Rule
diagnosticTests.purpose: Decides who needs cervical-spine radiography after blunt trauma; validated to safely avoid imaging in over 50% of stable alert adults
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician assesses age, mechanism, paraesthesia, and active rotation to 45 degrees each side. Performed in the emergency department or primary care, takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph of the cervical spine (AP, lateral, open-mouth odontoid)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes fracture, dislocation, and gross instability in WAD grade I-II
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three quick X-ray exposures with the patient sitting or standing. Total time 5-10 minutes; the report is usually available the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Preferred imaging in older adults, severe trauma, intoxicated patients, and where plain films are inadequate
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies flat in a CT scanner for 1-2 minutes. Result available within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI cervical spine with neural foramina
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects disc injury, nerve root compression, ligamentous tear, and spinal cord oedema in WAD grade III or persistent radicular symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in the MRI scanner for 25-40 minutes. No radiation. Result reported within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Neurological examination with reflex, dermatome, and myotome testing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by the treating clinician; takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Neck Disability Index and numeric pain rating scale at baseline and 6, 12 weeks
diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective measures of disability and pain; guide prognosis and treatment intensity

treatmentOverview: Management follows the Bone and Joint Decade Neck Pain Task Force 2008 recommendations and contemporary guidelines from NICE NG56 and Australian TRACsa: reassurance, encouragement to stay active, simple analgesia, and structured exercise. Soft cervical collars are explicitly not recommended beyond very short symptomatic use because they prolong recovery in randomised trials. The first 2-3 weeks should emphasise active mobilisation — gentle range-of-motion exercises, postural retraining, and return to usual activities. Paracetamol up to 4 g/day is first-line analgesia; NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) are added for moderate pain, with gastroprotection in patients at risk. Short courses of weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) may be used for severe acute pain but not beyond 1-2 weeks. Adjuncts include topical NSAIDs and heat or cold therapy. Physiotherapy with manual therapy, supervised exercise (targeting cranio-cervical flexors, scapular stabilisers, and global cervical strength), and education on pain neuroscience improves outcomes at 12 weeks in moderate-evidence trials. Patients with persistent moderate-severe symptoms at 6-12 weeks benefit from multimodal programmes including cognitive behavioural therapy targeting fear-avoidance and catastrophising, especially when post-traumatic stress symptoms are present. Diagnostic medial-branch blocks identify cervical facet-joint pain in chronic WAD; radiofrequency neurotomy of the medial branches produces 6-12 month relief in 60-80% of confirmed responders. Surgical fixation is reserved for WAD grade IV with confirmed unstable injury.

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1,000 mg up to four times daily (max 4 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia. Safe in pregnancy and most comorbidities; combine with activity advice and physiotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest single-agent effect but well tolerated; mainstay of combined regimens

medicalTreatments.name: Oral NSAID (ibuprofen 200-400 mg three times daily, or naproxen 250-500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Added for moderate pain not controlled by paracetamol. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Add a proton pump inhibitor in patients over 65 or with peptic ulcer history.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 30-50% pain reduction in acute neck pain RCTs; small additional benefit over paracetamol

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physiotherapy with active exercise and manual therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Supervised exercise targeting deep cervical flexors, scapulothoracic stabilisers, and proprioception, combined with manual therapy (mobilisation, soft-tissue techniques). Patient education on pain neuroscience reduces fear-avoidance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 10-20 point Neck Disability Index improvement versus advice alone at 12 weeks (Stewart 2007, Sterling 2014)

medicalTreatments.name: Short course of weak opioid (codeine 30 mg or tramadol 50-100 mg, 4-6 hourly, max 1-2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe acute pain unresponsive to paracetamol and NSAIDs. Combine with laxatives and review at 5-7 days. Avoid long-term use to prevent opioid dependence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adds 1-2 points pain reduction in the first week in acute neck pain trials; no benefit in chronic WAD

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy and graded activity programmes
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated in patients with persistent symptoms beyond 6-12 weeks, high catastrophising scores, or post-traumatic stress symptoms. Address fear-avoidance, pacing, and return to activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 20-30% greater improvement in disability versus usual care in chronic WAD trials

medicalTreatments.name: Cervical medial-branch radiofrequency neurotomy
medicalTreatments.description: Considered after positive controlled diagnostic medial-branch blocks in patients with chronic facet-joint-mediated WAD. Performed under fluoroscopy by interventional pain specialists.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median pain relief of 6-12 months in 60-80% of confirmed responders; repeat procedures often feasible

surgicalOptions.name: Cervical fixation/fusion for WAD grade IV with confirmed instability
surgicalOptions.description: Performed urgently for fracture-dislocations, unstable ligamentous injuries with neurological compromise, or progressive deformity. Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion is the most common approach.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Fusion rates above 90%; neurological recovery depends on severity and timing of presentation

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest short-term pain relief in chronic neck pain meta-analyses; may be considered as an adjunct when patients prefer it and standard care is insufficient.

alternativeTreatments.name: Manipulation and mobilisation by trained physiotherapists or chiropractors
alternativeTreatments.description: Mobilisation and grade 2-3 manipulation give short-term pain and range-of-motion improvements in low-grade WAD when delivered by trained practitioners. Avoid high-velocity manipulation in WAD grade III-IV or where vertebral artery dissection is a concern.

whySeeSpecialist: Most whiplash injuries are managed in primary care and physiotherapy. Specialist referral to physical medicine and rehabilitation or sports medicine is warranted for persistent symptoms beyond 6-12 weeks, suspected facet-joint pain, neurological signs, or chronic post-traumatic stress symptoms. Orthopaedic or neurosurgical referral is needed for WAD grade IV, persistent radicular signs, or imaging evidence of significant injury.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a physiotherapist with experience in cervical spine rehabilitation and graded activity programmes. For persistent symptoms, look for a rehabilitation physician or pain specialist who offers diagnostic medial-branch blocks and cognitive behavioural therapy in a multidisciplinary setting. Ask whether the clinic uses validated outcome measures (Neck Disability Index, numeric pain rating scale).
hospitalCriteria: Emergency department with 24/7 access to cervical CT
Physiotherapy service with cervical spine rehabilitation expertise
Pain management clinic offering medial-branch blocks and radiofrequency neurotomy
Multidisciplinary chronic pain service including clinical psychology
Neurosurgical and orthopaedic spinal services for severe injuries
keyFacilities: Emergency departments with major trauma capability
preventionStrategies: Adjust the headrest so its top edge is at or above the top of the head and within 5 cm of the back of the head.
Maintain at least 2 seconds of following distance when driving to allow braking and reduce delta-V in rear-end events.
Wear lap-and-shoulder seat belts on every car journey; modern integrated head restraints reduce whiplash claims by 20-30%.
Avoid driving when fatigued or after alcohol — alertness allows preparatory bracing that reduces injury severity.
Use appropriately fitted helmets and head protection in contact sports and combat sports.
Strengthen the neck and scapular muscles with regular resistance training in occupational groups at elevated risk (drivers, contact-sport athletes).
lifestyleModifications: Stay active in the first days after injury rather than resting in a collar; gentle movement promotes recovery.
Maintain usual sleep, work, and leisure as far as pain allows; staged return to work within 1-2 weeks is the norm.
Apply heat or cold packs to the neck for 15-20 minutes several times daily for symptomatic relief.
Use a supportive but not overly firm pillow that maintains neutral neck alignment during sleep.
Set short daily goals for range-of-motion exercises and progress them over 4-6 weeks.
Address sleep difficulty and low mood promptly — both delay recovery.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day during rehabilitation) to support muscle recovery
Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, leafy greens, berries, olive oil)
Adequate hydration to support tissue repair and reduce muscle cramping
Foods rich in vitamin D and calcium to support bone and ligament health
avoid: Excess alcohol, which delays soft-tissue healing and interferes with sleep
Heavy reliance on processed and ultra-processed foods which worsen systemic inflammation
Smoking and nicotine, which impair ligament and disc healing
Excess caffeine close to bedtime, which worsens sleep quality during recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Begin gentle cervical range-of-motion exercises within 24-72 hours of injury — slow rotation, flexion, extension, and lateral flexion to the limits of comfort, 10 repetitions three times daily. Progress over 2-4 weeks to deep cervical flexor activation (chin tucks), scapular retraction, and resistance band exercises. Resume aerobic activity (walking, cycling, swimming) at low intensity in the first week and build up over 4-6 weeks. Avoid contact sports until pain is settled, range of motion is full, and strength is restored.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed cervical mobility and strengthening exercises twice daily.
Apply heat or cold packs for symptomatic relief between exercise sessions.
Take paracetamol or NSAIDs at regular intervals during the first week rather than on demand to break the pain cycle.
Maintain good sleep hygiene with a supportive pillow and consistent bedtime.
Keep a pain and activity diary for the first 4 weeks to identify aggravating tasks.
Stay engaged with usual work and social activities — graded return reduces chronic disability.
prognosis: Around 50% of patients with WAD grade I-II recover fully within 3-6 months, and 70-75% by 12 months. Persistent symptoms beyond 12 months occur in 25-30% and define chronic WAD, a major cause of long-term neck pain and disability. The strongest predictors of chronic disability are high initial pain intensity (above 6/10), high Neck Disability Index (above 30/100) at baseline, post-traumatic stress symptoms, pre-existing psychological distress, and pre-collision neck pain. Mechanical factors such as collision speed and imaging findings have weaker prognostic value once initial neurological status is accounted for. Early reassurance, active rehabilitation, and avoidance of collars are associated with better outcomes. Multidisciplinary programmes addressing pain, psychological distress, and graded return to activity improve outcomes in 40-60% of those with established chronic WAD.
recoveryTimeline: Acute pain peaks within 24-72 hours, then improves over 2-4 weeks in most patients. Range of motion returns by 4-6 weeks. Most patients return to normal work and leisure activities within 4-8 weeks. Residual symptoms at 12 weeks predict chronic outcomes; multidisciplinary rehabilitation is recommended at this point. Patients with chronic WAD often have variable courses with partial recovery over 6-18 months on structured programmes.
complications: Chronic whiplash-associated disorder with persistent pain, reduced range of motion, and disability beyond 12 months in 25-30% of patients.
Cervicogenic headache and tension-type headache, sometimes daily, in 30-50% of patients in the first 3 months.
Post-traumatic stress disorder, depression, and anxiety in 15-25% of patients after motor vehicle collisions.
Chronic facet-joint pain confirmed by diagnostic medial-branch blocks in 25-50% of chronic WAD cases.
Cervical radiculopathy or disc herniation in a small proportion (WAD grade III); identified by persistent radicular pain and MRI findings.
Rarely: vertebral artery dissection presenting with severe occipital headache, vertigo, and posterior circulation stroke.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation — Whiplash patient information
supportResources.description: Specialty society with patient-facing information on whiplash and rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline NG56 — Multimorbidity and chronic pain
supportResources.description: UK clinical guidance relevant to acute and chronic neck pain management.

supportResources.name: Australian Whiplash and Spinal Pain Research Group
supportResources.description: Research group at Griffith University producing patient-focused guides on whiplash recovery.

supportResources.name: Bone and Joint Decade Neck Pain Task Force
supportResources.description: Authoritative international consensus on neck pain assessment and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Whiplash is a soft-tissue injury of the cervical spine with a defined acceleration-deceleration mechanism, neck pain, and reduced cervical range of motion. Generic back pain refers to the thoracic and lumbar spine without the inertial mechanism characteristic of whiplash.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Whiplash headache is steady, posterior, often unilateral or band-like, and provoked by neck movement. Migraine is throbbing, hemicranial, with photophobia, phonophobia, and nausea; it can be triggered or worsened by whiplash but follows a typical migraine pattern with aura in some patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic low back pain affects the lumbar spine and is rarely caused by whiplash injury. Chronic WAD involves the cervical spine and headache. Both share risk factors (psychological distress, catastrophising) but anatomical localisation differs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Raynaud Phenomenon
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Some patients report cold, tingling hands after whiplash. Raynaud phenomenon, by contrast, produces episodic colour change (white-blue-red) of the fingers with cold exposure or stress and is unrelated to cervical mechanism.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common musculoskeletal pain syndrome; shares biopsychosocial management principles with chronic WAD.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Some patients with chronic WAD develop widespread pain consistent with fibromyalgia; the conditions share central sensitisation features.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent comorbidity after whiplash; trigeminocervical convergence explains overlapping head and neck symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Pre-existing or post-collision depression worsens whiplash recovery and is targeted in multidisciplinary care.

relatedConditions.name: Anxiety
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-traumatic stress and generalised anxiety frequently follow road traffic collisions and predict chronic WAD.

faqs.question: What is whiplash?
faqs.answer: Whiplash is a soft-tissue injury of the neck caused by a sudden forced movement backwards and then forwards, most often during a rear-end car collision. It produces neck pain, stiffness, headache, and reduced range of motion within 24-48 hours of impact.

faqs.question: How long does whiplash last?
faqs.answer: Around 50% of patients recover within 3-6 months and 70-75% within 12 months. About 25-30% develop chronic symptoms lasting beyond a year, especially when initial pain is severe and psychological distress is high.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of whiplash?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include neck pain and stiffness, headache, shoulder and upper back pain, dizziness, fatigue, jaw pain, tingling in the arms, and reduced cervical range of motion. Symptoms typically appear 6-48 hours after the injury.

faqs.question: Do I need an X-ray for whiplash?
faqs.answer: Most uncomplicated whiplash injuries do not need imaging. The Canadian C-spine Rule helps decide who needs an X-ray or CT: age over 65, dangerous mechanism, paraesthesia, or inability to rotate the neck 45 degrees each side mandate imaging.

faqs.question: Should I wear a soft collar?
faqs.answer: Soft cervical collars are not recommended beyond very brief symptomatic use. Randomised trials show that prolonged collar use slows recovery compared with staying active and gentle exercise. Most guidelines now advise early mobilisation.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for whiplash?
faqs.answer: Best practice is reassurance, advice to stay active, simple analgesia (paracetamol with or without an NSAID), and structured physiotherapy with active exercise and manual therapy. Cognitive behavioural therapy helps patients with persistent symptoms and high distress.

faqs.question: Can whiplash cause long-term damage?
faqs.answer: Most patients recover fully. About 25-30% have persistent pain or disability beyond a year (chronic WAD). True structural damage requiring surgery is uncommon — only WAD grade IV with fracture or unstable ligamentous injury needs operative fixation.

faqs.question: Can whiplash cause dizziness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cervicogenic dizziness and unsteadiness occur in 20-30% of patients in the first month, typically without true vestibular signs. Persistent severe vertigo warrants ENT or neurology assessment to exclude vertebral artery injury.

faqs.question: What exercises help whiplash recovery?
faqs.answer: Gentle cervical range-of-motion exercises (rotation, flexion, extension, lateral flexion) within 24-72 hours of injury, progressing to chin tucks for deep cervical flexors, scapular retraction, and resistance band exercises by week 2-3 under physiotherapy guidance.

faqs.question: When should I return to work after whiplash?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to usual work within 1-2 weeks, sometimes with modified duties initially. Prolonged absence delays recovery. Heavy manual workers and drivers may need 4-6 weeks, especially with WAD grade II or persistent symptoms.

faqs.question: Is whiplash worse in women than men?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women have 1.5-2 times higher incidence and roughly 2 times higher risk of chronic symptoms, partly attributed to smaller cervical muscle mass, longer neck-to-thorax ratio, and possibly hormonal factors. Recovery strategies are otherwise similar to men.

faqs.question: Can whiplash cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cervicogenic and tension-type headaches occur in 50-70% of patients in the first month. Migraines can be triggered or worsened. Most headaches settle as the neck recovers, but persistent headache beyond 3 months warrants neurology and pain specialist review.

faqs.question: How does psychological state affect whiplash recovery?
faqs.answer: Pre-existing anxiety, depression, or PTSD doubles the risk of chronic whiplash. Catastrophising beliefs about the injury are the strongest psychological predictor of disability. Cognitive behavioural therapy alongside physiotherapy improves outcomes in 40-60% of patients with chronic WAD.

faqs.question: Can chiropractors help with whiplash?
faqs.answer: Mobilisation and low-force manipulation by trained practitioners give short-term pain and range-of-motion improvements in low-grade WAD. High-velocity manipulation should be avoided in WAD grade III-IV or where vertebral artery dissection is a concern.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for whiplash?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rare and limited to WAD grade IV with confirmed cervical fracture, dislocation, or unstable ligamentous injury, or to grade III with persistent radiculopathy and matching MRI findings. Most whiplash injuries do not require surgery.

faqs.question: Can children get whiplash?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is less common in children under 10 because of relative neck flexibility and lower mass. Adolescents and teenagers can sustain whiplash from contact sports and motor vehicle collisions. Recovery is usually faster than in working-age adults.

faqs.question: How can I prevent whiplash in a car accident?
faqs.answer: Adjust the headrest so its top edge is level with or above the top of your head and within 5 cm of the back. Wear a seat belt on every journey. Modern integrated head restraints and pre-crash bracing systems reduce whiplash claims by 20-30%.

faqs.question: Is whiplash covered by insurance?
faqs.answer: In most countries with motor third-party insurance, whiplash claims are covered. Coverage and award levels vary by jurisdiction. Litigation and protracted claims may slow recovery in some cohorts; early settlement and active rehabilitation favour better outcomes.

faqs.question: What is chronic whiplash?
faqs.answer: Chronic whiplash-associated disorder describes neck pain, stiffness, headache, and disability that persist beyond 3-6 months after the injury. It affects 25-30% of patients and is associated with high initial pain, psychological distress, and pre-existing neck pain.

faqs.question: Does radiofrequency neurotomy help chronic whiplash?
faqs.answer: Yes, in selected patients. After two positive controlled diagnostic medial-branch blocks, radiofrequency neurotomy of the cervical facet medial branches gives 6-12 months of pain relief in 60-80% of confirmed responders and can be repeated when pain returns.

faqs.question: Can whiplash cause TMJ problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 25% of patients with whiplash develop temporomandibular pain, clicking, or limited jaw opening, especially after high-energy impacts. Most settle with the same active rehabilitation approach used for neck symptoms; persistent TMJ symptoms warrant dental or orofacial pain specialist review.

metaTitle: Whiplash Injury: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Whiplash injury: neck pain, headache after car crash. WAD grades, Canadian C-spine rule, physiotherapy, and chronic neck pain treatment options explained.

sources.title: Spitzer WO, Skovron ML, Salmi LR, Cassidy JD, Duranceau J, Suissa S, Zeiss E. 1995. Scientific monograph of the Quebec Task Force on Whiplash-Associated Disorders. Spine 20(8 Suppl):1S-73S.

Carroll LJ, Holm LW, Hogg-Johnson S, et al. 2008. Course and prognostic factors for neck pain in whiplash-associated disorders (WAD): results of the Bone and Joint Decade 2000-2010 Task Force on Neck Pain and Its Associated Disorders. Spine 33(4S):S83-S92.
Stiell IG, Clement CM, McKnight RD, et al. 2003. The Canadian C-spine rule versus the NEXUS low-risk criteria in patients with trauma. New England Journal of Medicine 349(26):2510-2518.
Sterling M. 2014. Physiotherapy management of whiplash-associated disorders (WAD). Journal of Physiotherapy 60(1):5-12.
Bogduk N, Govind J. 2009. Medical management of acute and chronic low back pain — and whiplash. Best Practice & Research Clinical Rheumatology 23(2):241-251.
